event: Stone tools of the Anyathian culture indicate early human habitation in the central Irrawaddy valley.
Bronze Age communities at sites such as Nyaunggan develop metalworking and settled farming villages.
The Pyu peoples establish walled city-states such as Sri Ksetra and Beikthano in the Irrawaddy basin.
Pyu city-states flourish as Buddhist trading centers linking India and China along overland and river routes.
The Nanzhao kingdom raids and weakens the Pyu city-states, accelerating their decline.
The Burmans (Bamar) found the fortified settlement of Pagan (Bagan), seed of the future kingdom.
King Anawrahta ascends the throne and begins unifying the Irrawaddy valley into the Pagan Empire.
Anawrahta conquers the Mon kingdom of Thaton, importing Theravada Buddhism and scriptures to Pagan.
Mongol invasions under the Yuan dynasty shatter the Pagan Empire, ending its golden age.
The Mon-speaking Hanthawaddy kingdom rises in Lower Burma centered on Pegu (Bago).
The Ava (Inwa) kingdom is founded, becoming a leading Burman power in central Myanmar.
Queen Shin Sawbu begins her reign over the prosperous Mon kingdom of Hanthawaddy Pegu.
King Mingyinyo founds the Toungoo dynasty, which would go on to reunify Burma.
King Bayinnaung begins his reign, building the largest empire in Southeast Asian history.
Pegu falls and the First Toungoo Empire collapses amid Arakanese and Siamese pressure.
King Thalun moves the capital to Ava, consolidating the Restored Toungoo dynasty.
Mon forces sack Ava, ending the Toungoo dynasty and triggering a power struggle.
Alaungpaya founds the Konbaung dynasty, the last royal dynasty of Burma.
Konbaung armies sack the Siamese capital of Ayutthaya, marking the empire's expansionist peak.
King Bodawpaya conquers the kingdom of Arakan (Rakhine), absorbing it into Burma.
The First Anglo-Burmese War begins, marking the start of British colonial encroachment.
The Treaty of Yandabo ends the First Anglo-Burmese War, ceding Arakan and Tenasserim to Britain.
Britain annexes Lower Burma, including Rangoon and Pegu, after the Second Anglo-Burmese War.
The Third Anglo-Burmese War ends with the capture of Mandalay and the exile of King Thibaw.
Britain annexes all of Burma, making it a province of British India.
Burma is separated from British India and becomes a self-governing crown colony.
Japanese forces invade and occupy Burma during World War II with help from the Burma Independence Army.
Aung San and the Anti-Fascist organization switch sides to aid Allied forces in liberating Burma.
The Panglong Agreement is signed, promising autonomy to ethnic minorities within a unified Burma.
Independence leader Aung San and several cabinet colleagues are assassinated months before independence.
Burma gains full independence from Britain on January 4, becoming a sovereign republic.
Burma is admitted to the United Nations as a member state.
Burmese diplomat U Thant becomes Secretary-General of the United Nations.
General Ne Win seizes power in a military coup, launching the isolationist Burmese Way to Socialism.
A new constitution establishes a one-party socialist state under the Burma Socialist Programme Party.
The 8888 Uprising erupts as mass pro-democracy protests are brutally crushed by the military.
The ruling junta officially renames the country Myanmar and the capital Yangon.
Aung San Suu Kyi's National League for Democracy wins elections, but the junta refuses to cede power.
Aung San Suu Kyi is awarded the Nobel Peace Prize while under house arrest.
The military government relocates the national capital to the newly built city of Naypyidaw.
The Saffron Revolution, led by Buddhist monks, sweeps the country before being suppressed.
Cyclone Nargis devastates the Irrawaddy Delta, killing roughly 140,000 people.
A nominally civilian government begins political and economic reforms, easing decades of isolation.
The National League for Democracy wins a landslide in Myanmar's first openly contested election in 25 years.
A military crackdown drives over 700,000 Rohingya to flee to Bangladesh amid accusations of genocide.
The military stages a coup, detaining Aung San Suu Kyi and triggering nationwide protests and civil conflict.
